nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef slopes and outer reef walls, typically at depths of 3–30 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small crustaceans and zooplankton. In captivity, generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, and copepods. Multiple small feedings per day (3–5 times) are commonly advised, especially for newly introduced specimens. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods can help supplement nutrition.

avoidWith: fish that compete aggressively for food

summary: A vibrant haremic anthias from the Western Central Pacific, the Huchtii Anthias is generally considered reef-safe and tends to thrive in groups with one dominant male and several females. Males display yellowish-green bodies with orange cheek markings and electric-blue fin outlines.
careNotes:
The Huchtii Anthias (Pseudanthias huchtii) is a protogynous hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and the dominant female in a group will transition to male if the group's male is lost. It is commonly recommended to keep a single male with a harem of 5 or more females to distribute aggression; keeping two males together tends to result in intense fighting. Males grow considerably larger (up to ~12 cm) than females (~6 cm).

This species tends to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward conspecifics and similarly shaped fish competing for food. It is generally considered reef-safe with no documented harm to corals or sessile invertebrates.

Multiple small feedings daily (3–5 times) are commonly advised; specimens that go off food can decline rapidly. A well-established refugium that produces copepods and amphipods can provide beneficial supplemental live prey.

The species inhabits outer reef slopes in the wild (Moluccas and Philippines to Vanuatu, south to the Great Barrier Reef, and Palau), typically at 3–30 m depth. Note: one secondary source (saltwaterfish.com) listed the native range as the Western Indian Ocean/Red Sea, which appears inconsistent with FishBase and ReefApp data for this species — that range likely describes a different Pseudanthias species. The FishBase-confirmed range (Western Central Pacific) is used here.

Escape risk is considered high for anthias as a group; a tight-fitting lid is strongly advisable.

Source temperature ranges showed some spread: LiveAquaria stated 72–78°F (22–26°C), saltwaterfish.com stated 75–80°F (24–27°C), and FishBase indicated 22–28°C. The values used (23–27°C) represent an average across sources.
breedingNotes: Rarely reported bred in captivity. This species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite; sex change from female to male can occur in aquaria when the dominant male is removed. Captive breeding would require a mature harem setup and specialized larval-rearing conditions. Breeding difficulty in captivity has not been well documented.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters, diet, and temperament. Note: this source listed native range as Western Indian Ocean/Red Sea, which contradicts FishBase and ReefApp for this species; that range data was not used.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Western Central Pacific), adult male size (~12 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), and taxonomy. Family listed as Anthiadidae in current FishBase taxonomy.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm), minimum tank recommendation (400 L / 106 gal), diet, and reef-safe status. Confirmed Western Central Pacific native range.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely if ever bred in captivity. Protogynous hermaphrodite; dominant female transitions to male when the group's male is absent. No established captive breeding protocol documented.
